planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; the cultivar 'Winter King' is vastly superior to seed-grown trees for ornamental effect and berry production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade; green hawthorn — especially the cultivar 'Winter King' — is one of the finest native small trees for four-season ornamental value and wildlife habitat. In spring the tree is covered with small white flowers that are excellent bee forage; in fall the foliage turns burgundy-orange; and in winter the persistent glossy orange-red berries shine against bare branches well into February, providing critical food for cedar waxwings, robins, and thrushes. The tree has a vase-shaped form with slightly pendulous branches and is relatively free of the fireblight and cedar-apple rust that plague many hawthorns. The thorns are formidable — plant away from walkways. Excellent as a wildlife garden specimen or fall/winter focal point.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; parsley hawthorn tolerates more shade than most hawthorns and grows naturally as an understory tree in bottomland forests.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; parsley hawthorn is the most delicate and ornamental of the southeastern hawthorns, distinguished by its exquisitely cut, deeply lobed leaves that truly resemble flat-leaf parsley — giving it an unusually fine, lacy texture among native trees. White flower clusters in spring are followed by small red berries in fall that provide wildlife food. It grows naturally as an understory tree in moist bottomland forests and shaded river banks throughout the Southeast and tolerates shade better than most hawthorn species. The gray-brown bark flakes to reveal attractive smooth orange-tan underbark. An underused native tree that deserves much wider garden use for its ornamental leaf texture and wildlife value.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter or container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; a single tree is self-fertile and will produce fruit without a pollinizer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; southern crabapple is the native crabapple of the southeastern United States, producing a spectacular display of intensely fragrant pink flowers in early spring and small (1/2–1 inch) yellow-green to red crabapples in late summer and fall. The crabapples are too tart to eat fresh but make outstanding jelly, cider, and preserved fruit with excellent pectin content. The flowers are superb for native bees; cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, and deer consume the fruit through fall and winter. It tolerates the heat and humidity of zone 8–9 better than most crabapple cultivars and produces cleaner foliage with fewer disease problems in the warm South. A self-fertile tree, so only one is needed for fruit.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil at pond or stream margins; keep the rhizome at the soil surface, not buried. Blooms best in full sun but tolerates partial shade.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions at the soil surface in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; blue flag iris is the quintessential native wetland iris of the northeastern and central US and Canada, growing in marshes, bogs, wet meadows, and streambanks with bold violet-blue flowers veined with white and yellow in late spring. It is native across a vast range from Labrador to Virginia, making it more cold-hardy than the similar southern blue flag (I. virginica). The flowers provide nectar for specialist long-tongued native bees and bumblebees. Plant at the margin of a water garden, in a rain garden, or in any consistently moist location. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. The rhizomes spread slowly to form handsome colonies; divide every 4–5 years in fall to maintain vigor.
